hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (90 mg/100 g), high potassium (314 mg/100 g), and abundant omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA)
hypertension.guidance: Raw saba mackerel is suitable for hypertensive individuals due to its favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio and high EPA/DHA content, which improve endothelial function and reduce arterial stiffness per AHA guidelines; avoid salted, canned, or pickled saba preparations that can exceed 400 mg sodium/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (18.6 g/100 g); omega-3 fatty acids improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw saba mackerel produces no glycaemic impact due to zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content, and its omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) improve insulin sensitivity per ADA guidelines; avoid sweet glazes or teriyaki preparations that add significant sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA ~2.5 g/100 g estimated), low saturated fat (3.30 g/100 g), high vitamin B12 and vitamin D
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw saba mackerel is a cardioprotective food; its high EPA/DHA content reduces triglycerides, lowers LDL oxidation, and reduces cardiovascular event risk per ACC/AHA guidelines; grill or bake rather than deep-fry to preserve omega-3 integrity and avoid trans fat formation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) suppress TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines; saturated fat 3.30 g/100 g is below 5 g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saba mackerel is one of the richest dietary sources of omega-3 fatty acids, which directly suppress RA-relevant inflammatory cytokines per EULAR guidelines; aim for 2–3 servings per week and avoid deep-fried or salted preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saba mackerel (raw) is a fresh, uncooked marine oily fish belonging to the Scombridae family, widely consumed across Japan, East Asia, and Southeast Asia. The fish is characterised by its distinctive blue-green iridescent back, silver-white belly, and rich pinkish-grey to brownish flesh with a firm, oily texture and a pronounced savoury, slightly briny flavour. Per 100 g, raw saba mackerel provides approximately 205 kcal, 18.6 g of high-quality complete protein, 13.89 g of total fat (of which 3.30 g is saturated fat and approximately 2.5 g is omega-3 EPA/DHA), and zero carbohydrate. It is an exceptional source of vitamin B12 (8.71 mcg, over 360% of daily reference intake), vitamin D (16.1 mcg), selenium (44.1 mcg), and niacin (9.08 mg). The sodium content is low at 90 mg/100 g, while potassium is high at 314 mg/100 g, making it favourable for blood pressure management. Clinically, saba mackerel is recommended for cardiovascular disease prevention, hypertension management, and type 2 diabetes due to its omega-3 fatty acid content, which reduces triglycerides, improves endothelial function, and enhances insulin sensitivity. It is also beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its anti-inflammatory EPA/DHA profile. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. In Japanese cuisine, saba is commonly prepared as shioyaki (salt-grilled), simmered in miso (saba no misoni), or served as sashimi and sushi. It is also popular in Korean, Singaporean, and broader Southeast Asian cuisines. Raw saba is best stored on ice and consumed fresh or frozen promptly to prevent histamine formation.

confidence_reason: Primary macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for saba mackerel raw with confidence 0.95. Detailed fatty acid and micronutrient profile cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175097 (Atlantic mackerel, raw), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as saba mackerel is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175097. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal food product. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 confirmed per USDA FDC ID 175097. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable; glycemic_index field is set to numeric 0 per template numeric field requirement with gi_category not_applicable clarifying that the GI concept does not apply to this food. Saturated fat (3.30 g), monounsaturated fat (5.41 g), polyunsaturated fat (3.35 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and all micronutrient values (vitamin B12 8.71 mcg, vitamin D 16.1 mcg, selenium 44.1 mcg, phosphorus 217 mg, magnesium 76 mg, zinc 0.63 mg, water 63.55 g, vitamins A/B1/B2/B3/B6/C/E/folate) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 175097 (Atlantic mackerel, raw) as the closest approved-source equivalent for detailed nutrient profile not reported in HPB Foodo. The health_suitability entry for rheumatoid_arthritis in the input JSON listed suitable: false, which appears to be a data entry error given the supporting reason text clearly describes saba mackerel as beneficial for RA; this has been corrected to concern_level: low with appropriate clinical guidance. Saba mackerel is a known high-histamine risk food if not stored properly; consumers should ensure freshness. Mercury content is moderate; pregnant women should follow local advisory limits on oily fish consumption.
